nodes.label: General separation principles
nodes.summary: Separations exploit differences in physical/chemical properties such as polarity, size, charge, volatility, and solubility to isolate components of a mixture.

nodes.label: Property-based separation
nodes.summary: Each technique separates compounds by a specific distinguishing property.

nodes.label: Polarity differences
nodes.summary: Many separations (extraction, chromatography) rely on differences in molecular polarity governing affinity for phases.

nodes.label: Molecular size/weight differences
nodes.summary: Size-based methods (size-exclusion chromatography, SDS-PAGE, dialysis) separate by molecular dimensions or mass.

nodes.label: Charge differences
nodes.summary: Electrophoresis and ion-exchange chromatography separate species according to net charge.

nodes.label: Volatility/boiling point differences
nodes.summary: Distillation separates components by differences in vapor pressure and boiling point.

nodes.label: Solubility differences
nodes.summary: Recrystallization, extraction, and precipitation exploit differential solubility in chosen solvents.

nodes.label: Partitioning between phases
nodes.summary: A solute distributes between two phases according to its relative affinity, the foundation of extraction and chromatography.

nodes.label: Density differences
nodes.summary: Centrifugation and layer identification in extraction rely on differences in density/buoyancy.

nodes.label: Distribution (partition) coefficient
nodes.summary: The partition coefficient quantifies how a solute distributes between two immiscible solvents at equilibrium.

nodes.label: Partition coefficient K = [solute]organic/[solute]aqueous
nodes.summary: K equals the equilibrium concentration ratio of solute in the two phases and is temperature-dependent.

nodes.label: Like-dissolves-like rule
nodes.summary: Nonpolar solutes favor nonpolar (organic) solvents while polar/ionic solutes favor polar (aqueous) solvents.

nodes.label: Calculating amount extracted from K
nodes.summary: Given K and phase volumes, the fraction of solute remaining after one or more extractions can be calculated from the equilibrium distribution.

nodes.label: Assessing purity
nodes.summary: Purity is judged by criteria such as a sharp narrow melting range, a single spot, or a single peak.

nodes.label: Narrow melting range as purity test
nodes.summary: A pure crystalline solid melts over a narrow temperature range, while impurities broaden and lower the melting range.

nodes.label: Mixed melting point identification
nodes.summary: Mixing an unknown with a known authentic sample depresses and broadens the melting point unless they are identical, confirming identity.

nodes.label: Single chromatographic spot/peak
nodes.summary: A single TLC spot or single chromatographic peak across conditions is evidence of purity.

nodes.label: Percent recovery / yield
nodes.summary: Percent recovery = (mass recovered ÷ mass started) × 100; purification trades yield for purity, so recovery is below 100%.

nodes.label: Extraction
nodes.summary: Extraction separates compounds based on differential solubility between two immiscible liquid phases.

nodes.label: Liquid-liquid extraction
nodes.summary: A solute is partitioned between an aqueous and an immiscible organic solvent in a separatory funnel.

nodes.label: Separatory funnel technique
nodes.summary: Two immiscible layers are shaken, vented, allowed to separate, and the desired layer is drained off.

nodes.label: Immiscible solvent pair
nodes.summary: Extraction requires two solvents that do not mix, typically water and an organic solvent.

nodes.label: Common organic extraction solvents
nodes.summary: Diethyl ether and ethyl acetate are low-density solvents; dichloromethane and chloroform are dense, halogenated solvents.

nodes.label: Identifying organic vs aqueous layer by density
nodes.summary: The denser solvent forms the bottom layer; ether floats on water while chloroform/DCM sinks below it.

nodes.label: Solute partitioning during extraction
nodes.summary: A solute moves into the layer for which it has greater affinity, governed by the partition coefficient.

nodes.label: Multiple small extractions outperform one large
nodes.summary: Several extractions with small solvent portions recover more solute than a single extraction with the same total volume.

nodes.label: Washing vs extracting
nodes.summary: Extraction removes the desired product into a layer; washing removes unwanted impurities into the discarded layer.

nodes.label: Salting out
nodes.summary: Adding salt (e.g. NaCl) to the aqueous layer lowers organic-solute solubility in water, driving it into the organic layer and breaking emulsions.

nodes.label: Emulsion formation
nodes.summary: Vigorous shaking can form an emulsion (suspended droplets) that prevents clean layer separation.

nodes.label: Venting to release pressure
nodes.summary: Volatile solvents and gas-producing reactions build pressure that must be vented during extraction.

nodes.label: Acid-base extraction
nodes.summary: Adjusting pH converts compounds between neutral (organic-soluble) and ionic (water-soluble) forms to separate them.

nodes.label: pH controls protonation state
nodes.summary: Acids and bases are deprotonated/protonated by adjusting pH, switching their solubility between phases.

nodes.label: Charged species partition into water
nodes.summary: A charged (ionized) compound preferentially dissolves in the aqueous layer.

nodes.label: Neutral species partition into organic
nodes.summary: A neutral (uncharged) organic compound preferentially dissolves in the organic layer.

nodes.label: pKa vs pH determines ionization
nodes.summary: A group is mostly ionized when the pH is on the charged side of its pKa (Henderson-Hasselbalch), setting which layer it enters.

nodes.label: Acidic wash extracts amines
nodes.summary: Washing with aqueous acid (e.g. HCl) protonates an amine to its ammonium salt, pulling it into the aqueous layer.

nodes.label: Basic wash extracts carboxylic acids/phenols
nodes.summary: Washing with aqueous base (e.g. NaHCO3 or NaOH) deprotonates carboxylic acids/phenols to water-soluble salts.

nodes.label: NaHCO3 separates carboxylic acids from phenols
nodes.summary: Weak base sodium bicarbonate deprotonates strong carboxylic acids but not weaker phenols, allowing their separation.

nodes.label: Back-extraction by re-neutralization
nodes.summary: Re-adjusting pH reconverts a salt to the neutral compound so it can be extracted back into fresh organic solvent.

nodes.label: Solid-liquid extraction
nodes.summary: A soluble component is dissolved out of a solid mixture by a chosen solvent.

nodes.label: Selective dissolution from a solid
nodes.summary: A solvent dissolves the desired component while leaving insoluble solids behind (e.g. brewing coffee).

nodes.label: Continuous (Soxhlet) extraction
nodes.summary: A Soxhlet apparatus repeatedly recycles fresh distilled solvent through a solid sample for exhaustive extraction with little solvent.

nodes.label: Drying agents
nodes.summary: Anhydrous salts remove residual water from an organic layer after extraction.

nodes.label: Anhydrous salts remove trace water
nodes.summary: Drying agents such as MgSO4 or Na2SO4 absorb dissolved water from an organic solution.

nodes.label: Free-flowing solid signals dryness
nodes.summary: Enough drying agent has been added when the solid swirls freely rather than clumping; it is then removed by filtration before solvent evaporation.

nodes.label: Distillation
nodes.summary: Distillation separates liquids by differences in boiling point through vaporization and selective condensation.

nodes.label: Principles of distillation
nodes.summary: A liquid is heated to its boiling point, vaporized, and condensed back to a purer liquid.

nodes.label: Boiling point set by vapor pressure
nodes.summary: A liquid boils when its vapor pressure equals the external (atmospheric) pressure.

nodes.label: Vapor is enriched in the volatile component
nodes.summary: The vapor above a boiling mixture is richer in the lower-boiling (more volatile) component.

nodes.label: Raoult's law for ideal mixtures
nodes.summary: Each component's partial vapor pressure equals its mole fraction times its pure vapor pressure (P_A = X_A·P°_A).

nodes.label: Boiling chips prevent bumping
nodes.summary: Boiling chips (or stir bars) provide nucleation sites for smooth boiling and prevent superheating and violent bumping.

nodes.label: Simple distillation
nodes.summary: Simple distillation separates liquids whose boiling points differ widely (roughly > 25 °C).

nodes.label: Used for large boiling-point differences
nodes.summary: Simple distillation is effective only when components' boiling points differ by roughly 25 °C or more.

nodes.label: Simple distillation apparatus
nodes.summary: The setup uses a heated flask, thermometer at the side-arm, condenser, and collection (receiving) flask.

nodes.label: Thermometer at the still-head
nodes.summary: The thermometer bulb is positioned at the side-arm opening to read the temperature of the vapor being collected.

nodes.label: Condenser cools vapor to liquid
nodes.summary: A water-cooled condenser removes heat so vapor condenses back to liquid distillate flowing into the receiving flask.

nodes.label: Fractional distillation
nodes.summary: A fractionating column enables separation of liquids with close boiling points.

nodes.label: Used for close boiling points
nodes.summary: Fractional distillation separates liquids whose boiling points differ by less than ~25 °C.

nodes.label: Fractionating column provides many vaporization-condensation cycles
nodes.summary: The packed column creates repeated vaporization-condensation equilibria (theoretical plates) that progressively enrich the vapor.

nodes.label: Theoretical plates
nodes.summary: Each effective vaporization-condensation cycle is a theoretical plate; more plates give better separation.

nodes.label: Industrial fractionation of crude oil
nodes.summary: Crude oil is separated into fractions (gas, gasoline, kerosene, diesel) by boiling-point range in a fractionating tower.

nodes.label: Vacuum distillation
nodes.summary: Reduced pressure lowers boiling points, allowing distillation of high-boiling or heat-sensitive compounds.

nodes.label: Lowering pressure lowers boiling point
nodes.summary: Reducing external pressure lets a liquid boil at a lower temperature, avoiding thermal decomposition.

nodes.label: Used for high-boiling/heat-sensitive compounds
nodes.summary: Vacuum distillation is chosen when a compound boils above ~150 °C or decomposes near its normal boiling point.

nodes.label: Steam distillation
nodes.summary: Steam distillation co-distills water-immiscible volatile compounds below 100 °C, used for temperature-sensitive natural products.

nodes.label: Immiscible vapor pressures add (Dalton)
nodes.summary: For two immiscible liquids each exerts its full vapor pressure independently, so the mixture boils when the sum equals atmospheric pressure—below either pure boiling point.

nodes.label: Isolating essential oils / natural products
nodes.summary: Steam distillation isolates fragrant, heat-sensitive, water-immiscible compounds (e.g. essential oils) that would decompose at their normal boiling point.

nodes.label: Rotary evaporation
nodes.summary: A rotovap removes volatile solvent from a solution under reduced pressure with a rotating heated flask.

nodes.label: Gentle solvent removal under vacuum
nodes.summary: Reduced pressure and a warm rotating flask evaporate solvent quickly at low temperature, concentrating a dissolved product.

nodes.label: Azeotropes
nodes.summary: An azeotrope is a mixture that distills at constant composition and cannot be further separated by ordinary distillation.

nodes.label: Constant-boiling mixture
nodes.summary: At the azeotrope, vapor and liquid have identical compositions, so distillation cannot enrich either component further.

nodes.label: Minimum- vs maximum-boiling azeotropes
nodes.summary: Positive deviation from Raoult's law gives a minimum-boiling azeotrope; negative deviation gives a maximum-boiling azeotrope.

nodes.label: Ethanol-water azeotrope (95% EtOH)
nodes.summary: Ethanol-water forms a ~95.6% ethanol azeotrope that ordinary distillation cannot exceed.

nodes.label: Chromatography
nodes.summary: Chromatography separates components by their differential partitioning between a stationary and a mobile phase.

nodes.label: General chromatographic principles
nodes.summary: Components migrate at different rates based on relative affinity for the stationary versus mobile phase.

nodes.label: Stationary vs mobile phase
nodes.summary: All chromatography uses a fixed stationary phase and a moving mobile phase that carries the sample through it.

nodes.label: Separation by differential affinity
nodes.summary: Components with greater affinity for the stationary phase move more slowly than those favoring the mobile phase.

nodes.label: Normal-phase vs reverse-phase
nodes.summary: Normal phase uses a polar stationary phase (polar compounds retained); reverse phase uses a nonpolar stationary phase (nonpolar compounds retained).

nodes.label: Elution order from polarity
nodes.summary: In normal-phase chromatography, less polar compounds elute first because they spend more time in the mobile phase.

nodes.label: Reverse-phase elution order
nodes.summary: On a nonpolar (reverse-phase) stationary phase, polar compounds elute first and nonpolar compounds are retained longer.

nodes.label: Resolution between components
nodes.summary: Resolution measures how completely two components are separated; better separation gives sharper, more widely spaced bands or peaks.

nodes.label: Paper chromatography
nodes.summary: Paper chromatography separates compounds on cellulose paper as solvent ascends by capillary action.

nodes.label: Cellulose/bound water stationary phase
nodes.summary: The stationary phase is polar (cellulose with adsorbed water), so polar compounds are retained and migrate less, analogous to normal-phase TLC.

nodes.label: Thin-layer chromatography (TLC)
nodes.summary: TLC separates compounds on a thin silica/alumina plate as solvent rises by capillary action.

nodes.label: TLC plate and developing chamber
nodes.summary: A spotted plate is placed in a sealed chamber so the mobile-phase solvent ascends by capillary action.

nodes.label: Silica/alumina stationary phase is polar
nodes.summary: The polar silica or alumina stationary phase retains polar compounds via hydrogen bonding and dipole interactions.

nodes.label: Retention factor Rf
nodes.summary: Rf = distance traveled by spot ÷ distance traveled by solvent front, a characteristic value under fixed conditions.

nodes.label: Polar compounds have lower Rf (normal phase)
nodes.summary: In normal-phase TLC, more polar compounds bind the silica and travel less, giving a smaller Rf.

nodes.label: Increasing mobile-phase polarity raises Rf
nodes.summary: A more polar solvent elutes polar compounds faster, increasing their Rf in normal-phase TLC.

nodes.label: Co-spotting with known standards
nodes.summary: Spotting an unknown alongside a reference; matching Rf (and a single spot when co-spotted) supports identity.

nodes.label: Visualization of TLC spots
nodes.summary: Spots are visualized with UV light, iodine vapor, or chemical stains since many compounds are colorless.

nodes.label: Column chromatography
nodes.summary: Column chromatography passes mobile phase through a packed column to separate larger sample amounts.

nodes.label: Gravity and flash column chromatography
nodes.summary: Mobile phase flows down a silica-packed column under gravity or applied pressure (flash) to separate components.

nodes.label: Nonpolar compounds elute first (normal phase)
nodes.summary: On a polar silica column, less polar compounds elute first and polar compounds are retained longer.

nodes.label: Fractions collected over time
nodes.summary: Eluent is collected in sequential fractions that separate the resolved components.

nodes.label: Gradient elution
nodes.summary: Gradually increasing mobile-phase polarity (gradient) elutes increasingly retained, more polar components in sequence.

nodes.label: Size-exclusion (gel-filtration) chromatography
nodes.summary: Size-exclusion chromatography separates molecules by size using a porous bead matrix.

nodes.label: Large molecules elute first
nodes.summary: Large molecules cannot enter the beads' pores and pass quickly, while small molecules are delayed by entering pores.

nodes.label: Porous bead stationary phase
nodes.summary: The stationary phase is a gel of porous beads whose pore size sets the size cutoff for separation.

nodes.label: Ion-exchange chromatography
nodes.summary: Ion-exchange chromatography separates charged molecules by their attraction to a charged stationary phase.

nodes.label: Charged resin binds opposite charges
nodes.summary: A charged column resin retains oppositely charged analytes: cation exchangers bind cations, anion exchangers bind anions.

nodes.label: Cation vs anion exchanger
nodes.summary: A cation-exchange resin carries fixed negative groups (binds positive analytes); an anion-exchange resin carries fixed positive groups (binds negative analytes).

nodes.label: Elution by salt gradient or pH change
nodes.summary: Bound molecules are released by increasing salt concentration or changing pH to alter their charge.

nodes.label: Affinity chromatography
nodes.summary: Affinity chromatography isolates a target using a specific binding partner immobilized on the column.

nodes.label: Immobilized ligand binds target specifically
nodes.summary: A ligand (antibody, substrate, receptor) bound to the resin selectively retains only its binding partner.

nodes.label: Elution with free ligand or competitive agent
nodes.summary: The target is released by washing with free ligand or changing conditions to break the specific interaction.

nodes.label: His-tag / Ni-NTA affinity purification
nodes.summary: A polyhistidine tag binds a nickel column and elutes with imidazole, a common recombinant-protein purification.

nodes.label: Gas chromatography (GC)
nodes.summary: Gas chromatography separates volatile compounds carried by an inert gas through a column.

nodes.label: Used for volatile compounds
nodes.summary: GC requires analytes that are volatile and thermally stable so they can be vaporized into the gas mobile phase.

nodes.label: Inert gas mobile phase
nodes.summary: An inert carrier gas (helium or nitrogen) transports vaporized analytes through the heated column.

nodes.label: Retention time
nodes.summary: Retention time is the characteristic time for a component to traverse the column and reach the detector.

nodes.label: GC-MS detector coupling
nodes.summary: Coupling GC to a mass spectrometer (GC-MS) both separates and identifies components by mass spectrum.

nodes.label: High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)
nodes.summary: HPLC uses high pressure to push liquid mobile phase through a tightly packed column for high-resolution separation.

nodes.label: High pressure improves resolution
nodes.summary: HPLC forces solvent through a fine-particle column under high pressure, giving fast, high-resolution separations.

nodes.label: Recrystallization and sublimation
nodes.summary: Crystallization-based methods purify solids using differential solubility and direct solid-vapor transitions.

nodes.label: Recrystallization
nodes.summary: Recrystallization purifies a solid by dissolving it hot and slowly cooling to form pure crystals.

nodes.label: Solubility increases with temperature
nodes.summary: The compound is dissolved in minimal hot solvent in which its solubility rises sharply with temperature.

nodes.label: Slow cooling yields pure crystals
nodes.summary: Slow cooling forms an ordered crystal lattice that excludes impurities, which remain dissolved in the cold solvent.

nodes.label: Ideal recrystallization solvent
nodes.summary: A good solvent dissolves the compound when hot but not when cold and keeps impurities soluble at all temperatures.

nodes.label: Mixed-solvent recrystallization
nodes.summary: When no single solvent works, a soluble solvent is paired with a miscible antisolvent to tune solubility for crystal formation.

nodes.label: Impurities excluded from lattice
nodes.summary: As crystals grow, the regular lattice rejects differently shaped/sized impurity molecules.

nodes.label: Hot filtration removes insoluble impurities
nodes.summary: Filtering the hot saturated solution removes insoluble impurities before cooling, while keeping the product dissolved.

nodes.label: Decolorizing with activated charcoal
nodes.summary: Activated carbon adsorbs colored/highly polar impurities from the hot solution and is removed by hot filtration.

nodes.label: Seeding/scratching induces nucleation
nodes.summary: Adding a seed crystal or scratching the flask provides nucleation sites that initiate crystallization from a supersaturated solution.

nodes.label: Sublimation
nodes.summary: Sublimation purifies a solid that converts directly to vapor and re-deposits without melting.

nodes.label: Direct solid-to-vapor transition
nodes.summary: A sublimable solid passes directly to the gas phase and re-condenses on a cold surface, leaving nonvolatile impurities.

nodes.label: Filtration and centrifugation
nodes.summary: These methods separate components based on physical phase boundaries, particle size, or density.

nodes.label: Filtration
nodes.summary: Filtration separates a solid from a liquid by passing the mixture through a porous barrier.

nodes.label: Gravity filtration
nodes.summary: Gravity filtration through filter paper is used when the desired product is the liquid (filtrate).

nodes.label: Vacuum (suction) filtration
nodes.summary: Vacuum filtration through a Büchner funnel speeds collection and is used when the desired product is the solid.

nodes.label: Residue vs filtrate
nodes.summary: The solid retained on the filter is the residue; the liquid that passes through is the filtrate.

nodes.label: Centrifugation
nodes.summary: Centrifugation separates components by density using strong centrifugal force.

nodes.label: Separation by density and mass
nodes.summary: Spinning sediments denser/larger particles to the bottom (pellet) while less dense material stays in the supernatant.

nodes.label: Pellet vs supernatant
nodes.summary: The sedimented solid is the pellet and the overlying liquid is the supernatant.

nodes.label: Differential centrifugation
nodes.summary: Successive spins at increasing speed pellet progressively smaller/lighter organelles, fractionating a cell lysate.

nodes.label: Density-gradient centrifugation
nodes.summary: Components band at positions matching their buoyant density within a preformed density gradient (e.g. sucrose, CsCl).

nodes.label: Ultracentrifugation and the Svedberg unit
nodes.summary: Very high-speed ultracentrifugation separates macromolecules by sedimentation coefficient (S, Svedberg), which depends on size, shape, and density.

nodes.label: Dialysis
nodes.summary: Dialysis separates small molecules from large ones across a semipermeable membrane by diffusion.

nodes.label: Semipermeable membrane size cutoff
nodes.summary: Small solutes diffuse across the membrane down their concentration gradient while large molecules are retained.

nodes.label: Electrophoresis
nodes.summary: Electrophoresis separates charged macromolecules in a gel under an electric field by size and charge.

nodes.label: Principles of gel electrophoresis
nodes.summary: An electric field drives charged molecules through a sieving gel matrix toward an oppositely charged electrode.

nodes.label: Migration toward opposite electrode
nodes.summary: Negatively charged molecules migrate toward the positive anode and positively charged molecules toward the negative cathode.

nodes.label: Gel sieves by size
nodes.summary: The gel matrix retards larger molecules, so for equal charge density smaller molecules migrate farther.

nodes.label: Mobility depends on charge-to-mass ratio
nodes.summary: Electrophoretic mobility increases with charge and decreases with size (frictional drag).

nodes.label: Running buffer maintains pH and conducts current
nodes.summary: The buffer holds a constant pH (fixing analyte charge) and carries the current between electrodes during the run.

nodes.label: Agarose gel for nucleic acids
nodes.summary: DNA/RNA fragments separate by size on an agarose gel since the phosphate backbone gives uniform negative charge.

nodes.label: Uniform negative charge of nucleic acids
nodes.summary: The phosphate backbone gives DNA a constant charge-to-mass ratio, so separation depends only on size.

nodes.label: Smaller fragments travel farther
nodes.summary: Shorter DNA fragments move through the gel pores faster and migrate farther from the wells.

nodes.label: Molecular-weight ladder/markers
nodes.summary: A reference ladder of known-size fragments run alongside the sample lets unknown band sizes be estimated.

nodes.label: SDS-PAGE for proteins
nodes.summary: SDS-PAGE separates denatured proteins by size by masking their intrinsic charge with SDS.

nodes.label: SDS denatures and coats proteins
nodes.summary: The detergent SDS denatures proteins and coats them with negative charge proportional to length, masking native charge.

nodes.label: Separation by size alone
nodes.summary: Because SDS gives a uniform charge-to-mass ratio, proteins separate purely by molecular weight (smaller = farther).

nodes.label: Reducing agent breaks disulfide bonds
nodes.summary: A reducing agent (e.g. β-mercaptoethanol or DTT) cleaves disulfide bonds to fully linearize subunits.

nodes.label: Protein staining (Coomassie/silver)
nodes.summary: Separated protein bands are visualized by Coomassie blue or more sensitive silver staining after the run.

nodes.label: Native PAGE
nodes.summary: Native PAGE separates proteins in their folded state by size, shape, and native charge together.

nodes.label: Proteins remain folded
nodes.summary: Without SDS, proteins keep their native conformation and charge, so migration reflects size, shape, and charge.

nodes.label: Isoelectric focusing (IEF)
nodes.summary: Isoelectric focusing separates proteins by their isoelectric point along a pH gradient.

nodes.label: Proteins migrate to their pI
nodes.summary: In a pH gradient each protein migrates until it reaches the pH equal to its isoelectric point, where its net charge is zero and it stops.

nodes.label: Net charge zero at pI
nodes.summary: At its pI a protein carries no net charge, so the electric field no longer drives its migration.

nodes.label: 2D gel electrophoresis
nodes.summary: 2D electrophoresis combines IEF then SDS-PAGE to resolve complex protein mixtures by pI and size.

nodes.label: IEF first dimension, SDS-PAGE second
nodes.summary: Proteins are separated by pI in the first dimension, then by molecular weight in the perpendicular second dimension.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Amino acid isoelectric point (pI) and titration
Nucleic acid structure and phosphodiester backbone
Disulfide bonds in protein tertiary/quaternary structure
